entry.title: Re: BRF+ S4/HANA 2025 BADI code ABAP for key users
entry.content:
Hi

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud ABAP Environment and ABAP Cloud (including Private Edition with key-user extensibility), direct usage of classic BRF+ runtime classes like CL_FDT_FUNCTION_PROCESS is not permitted due to strict ABAP cloud restrictions. This class is part of the classic ABAP stack and is not released for use in ABAP Cloud.

Explanation:

The SD_APM_SET_APPROVAL_REASON BAdI enforces ABAP Cloud compliance, which means:

  • You can only use released APIs.
  • You cannot instantiate or inherit from classic framework classes like CL_FDT_FUNCTION_PROCESS.
  • Most of BRF+ runtime classes are not released for ABAP Cloud usage.

Solutions and Workarounds:

1. Use a Released BRF+ Wrapper Interface (If Available)

In S/4HANA Cloud, sometimes the application provides its own wrapper to call BRF+ functions (via released APIs or CDS-based decisions). Check if there's a released interface or API view available to execute the BRF+ function indirectly.

Search in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE or /n/IWBEP/REG_SERVICE for any exposed services related to your BRF+ function. If your function is used by a standard scenario, it may already be exposed via a CDS view or service that you can consume.

2. Decision Table via Determination BAdI

Consider decoupling the logic:

  • Instead of calling BRF+ directly in the BAdI, maintain your logic in a custom helper class or a separate BAdI implementation (if permitted) that reads a decision table from a custom table or configuration.
  • Populate the decision table with entries mimicking the BRF+ rules.

This mimics BRF+ behavior in a cloud-compliant way.

3. Rebuild BRF+ Logic in CDS or Released ABAP Class

If BRF+ is only used for a small decision logic, rebuild the rules using a released CDS view with conditions or a utility class using if-else logic.

4. Use Determination via BTP

If your company has SAP BTP ABAP Environment, you can consume BRF+ logic exposed via BTP APIs or implement the decision logic there and call it via HTTP service (whitelisting required).

Summary:

In the ABAP Cloud context:

  • CL_FDT_FUNCTION_PROCESS and similar are not allowed.
  • BRF+ execution must be done via released artifacts.
  • Rebuild your decision logic in a compliant way (CDS view, utility class, custom config table, or wrapper service).
  • Explore whether SAP standard exposes your BRF+ logic via any public CDS or API.

Bedt regards

entry.title: Re: DM - POD error with new user
entry.content:
Dear Isabel

This issue is common in SAP Digital Manufacturing (DM) when new users are added but encounter authorization or assignment errors in the Work Center POD, especially when using the activity confirmation plugin.

Here are the likely causes and steps to resolve:

1. Check User Assignment at the Resource Level (Not Just Work Center)

Even if users are assigned to the work center, DM often validates user assignments at the resource level. Ensure:

  • The user is assigned to the specific resource used in the POD.
  • Go to Manage Resources → open the resource used in the POD → check the "Authorized Users" section and make sure the new users are listed.

2. User Group and Role Consistency

Verify the roles and user groups of the new users:

  • Compare them with the old working users.
  • Ensure roles like Production Operator, Activity Confirmation User, or any custom role with necessary permissions are assigned.
  • Go to Manage User Assignments or Manage Business Users and cross-verify the role templates.

3. Work Center vs Work Center Version

In some configurations, the assignment must be done to the active Work Center Version rather than the base work center. Ensure the version in use is active and the assignment applies to it.

4. Replication and Sync

If user or work center data is coming from S/4HANA or another upstream system:

  • Make sure that master data replication has occurred fully.
  • Use Monitor Data Synchronization to check if there are sync issues.

5. POD Configuration – Cached Data

Sometimes, PODs cache previous data. Restart the POD or clear cache:

  • Log out and back in.
  • Redeploy the POD if needed using Manage POD Designer.

6. Time Zone or Validity Settings

Check the validity period of the user assignment and the time zone settings. If the assignment is valid in the past or future but not the current time, it may result in this error.

7. Log and Trace

Use Application Logs (Monitor → Logs) in SAP Digital Manufacturing to get the exact error ID or cause. This can help confirm if it's an authorization issue or a missing assignment.

Recommendation

If all settings look correct and the issue persists:

  • Temporarily clone a working user and test.
  • Raise a support ticket with SAP and include:
    • The exact error message and timestamp.
    • The user ID and resource ID.
    • POD and plugin name.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: SAP XMII
entry.content:
Hi

SAP MII (Manufacturing Integration and Intelligence) and SAP ME (Manufacturing Execution) reaching end of mainstream support by December 31, 2030, signals a significant shift for manufacturing organizations relying on these platforms.

Transition Planning and Industry Response

Many organizations are beginning to evaluate their strategy now to avoid last-minute disruption. The two main paths most companies are considering are:

1. Transition to SAP Digital Manufacturing (SAP DM)

SAP DM (including Digital Manufacturing for Execution and Insights) is SAP’s strategic cloud-based replacement for MII and ME. It offers:

  • Native integration with S/4HANA Cloud and Private Edition.
  • Cloud-first architecture with scalable microservices.
  • Modern UI via POD Designer and out-of-the-box integration with shop floor systems.
  • Embedded analytics and real-time KPIs via Digital Manufacturing Insights (DMI).

This is the recommended route by SAP for greenfield or cloud-ready organizations.

Challenges:

  • Organizations with deep customization in MII need to redesign workflows in SAP DM.
  • Cloud infrastructure and data residency regulations may impact deployment.
  • Initial skill gaps with new architecture and tools.

Successes:

  • Some early adopters have reported faster deployment, improved UI, and reduced custom code maintenance.

2. Stay on SAP ME/MII Temporarily with Extended Maintenance

Some customers with heavy investment in custom MII/ME environments are opting to:

  • Stay on the existing stack until 2030.
  • Use this period to gradually modernize, migrate modules incrementally, or integrate with SAP DM as a hybrid.

Challenges:

  • Risk of unsupported systems beyond 2030.
  • Increasing technical debt and integration effort with newer SAP platforms.

3. Third-Party MES Options

A few manufacturers are evaluating or continuing with third-party MES platforms (e.g., Rockwell, Siemens, GE), integrating them with S/4HANA via BTP, PI/PO, or REST interfaces. This route may make sense in specialized industries, but typically involves higher integration and maintenance overhead.

Our Recommendations

  1. Start Assessment Now:

    • Conduct a fit-gap analysis between your MII/ME setup and SAP DM.
    • Identify critical custom scenarios and how they can be rebuilt in the new architecture.

  2. Engage with SAP Early:

    • Participate in SAP’s Customer Engagement Initiatives (CEI).
    • Leverage the migration guides, reference architectures, and BTP capabilities SAP provides.

  3. Invest in Skill Transition:

    • Train your teams on POD Designer, DM APIs, and Integration Suite.
    • Build internal prototypes and pilot scenarios in SAP DM.

  4. Consider Phased Migration:

    • Transition module by module, e.g., start with production reporting or quality inspection.
    • Use SAP BTP as a bridge for data orchestration during hybrid phases.

Final Thought

This transition isn’t just a technical shift—it’s a strategic opportunity to modernize manufacturing operations. Organizations that move early will not only avoid disruption but also unlock the real-time, cloud-native potential of SAP’s next-generation manufacturing solutions.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: How to Check View Counts for AM in sac add-in for excel
entry.content:
Dear Lora

Currently, SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and the SAC add-in for Microsoft Excel do not provide a built-in administrator menu or standard report specifically showing how often each Analytical Model (AM) from SAP Datasphere has been accessed or viewed within the Excel add-in.

Available Monitoring Options:

1. SAC Usage Tracking (General SAC Monitoring)

SAC offers usage tracking through:

  • System → Monitor → Usage Statistics
    This shows high-level activity like who accessed which story or model in SAC—but it does not track Excel add-in usage.

2. Audit Log in SAP Analytics Cloud

If enabled:

  • The audit log may provide insight into user logins and API usage, but it lacks granularity for AM views inside Excel.

3. SAP Datasphere Monitoring

SAP Datasphere has monitoring tools under:

  • System → Monitor → Data Access Statistics
    This shows query usage statistics per space, user, and time window. If an AM is queried frequently (via SAC or Excel), the backend activity will be visible here.

But:

  • You won’t see the access coming specifically from SAC Excel Add-in, only that the AM was queried.

4. Custom Logging (Advanced Option)

For more detailed tracking:

  • You can build a custom logging mechanism via SAC or Datasphere by monitoring:
    • Query logs
    • API calls
    • CDS view hits (if using CDS-based AMs)

Summary

There is no native admin dashboard in SAC Excel add-in to show per-AM view counts. Your best bet is to use SAP Datasphere’s Data Access Monitoring to track backend AM usage and correlate with user activity, or explore audit logs in SAC if enabled.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: Copying Custom Fields from Sales Orders to Purchase O...
entry.content:
Dear Edmund

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, direct enhancements or classic user exits are not available like in the on-premise version. The extensibility is limited to what SAP provides via released extension points and key-user tools.

In your case, the requirement is to copy a custom field from the Sales Order (SO) to the Purchase Order (PO) when the PO is created automatically (e.g., third-party process or intercompany scenario). Unfortunately, SAP only supports copying Texts by default between SO and PO in Public Cloud.

Available Options in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud:

1. Use Custom Fields with Adaptation and BAdI

  • Create the custom field in both the Sales Order and the Purchase Order using the Custom Fields app (/nSCFD_EUI).
  • Assign the custom field to the relevant business contexts:
    • Sales Document (e.g., SD_SALES_DOCUMENT)
    • Purchase Order (e.g., MM_PUR_PURCHASE_ORDER)
  • Once the fields are added and enabled for APIs and UI, use the BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PO_CREATE or MM_PUR_S4_PO_MODIFY to transfer data into the PO during creation.

However, these BAdIs are limited and do not provide access to the SO directly. So this may not meet your full requirement.

2. Use Side-by-Side Extension (BTP)

  • Build a side-by-side extension on SAP BTP using SAP Cloud SDK or CAP.
  • Listen to sales order events using Event Mesh (e.g., sales order created).
  • Fetch the SO with custom fields using OData or API.
  • Then update or enrich the PO via the Purchase Order API (API_PURCHASEORDER_PROCESS_SRV).

This approach allows full control but requires more development effort and BTP runtime.

3. Use Custom Logic (if extension point exists)

Check if SAP provides an in-app BAdI (Custom Logic app) in the Purchase Order creation scenario with a source reference to the sales order.

  • Go to "Custom Logic" app
  • Search for relevant BAdIs in the Purchase Order creation or item data determination.
  • If available, fetch the sales order reference (if passed in input) and assign the custom field.

SAP may not provide full SO context in the PO BAdI, so this is use-case dependent.

Summary

In SAP Public Cloud, there's no standard way to copy custom fields from SO to PO directly during the automatic creation process. You have two main paths:

  1. Custom Fields + BAdIs (limited, and may not have SO context).
  2. Side-by-side extension on BTP (flexible, but requires development and integration effort).

Best regards

entry.title: Re: SAP Signavio AI units
entry.content:
Dear Angelik

You're correct — SAP Signavio AI capabilities (such as Process Insights, Process Intelligence, or AI-Powered Recommendations) are provided through AI Units, and these require separate licensing from the standard Process Manager or Collaboration Hub licenses.

Key Points:

  1. AI Units Are Licensed Separately

    • AI Units are not included in your existing Signavio Process Manager or Collaboration Hub licenses.
    • You need to purchase them separately via your SAP account executive.

  2. License Model Is Usage-Based

    • AI Units are typically sold in bundles and are consumed based on the volume and type of AI analysis or processing (e.g., number of process insights, cases analyzed, etc.).
    • One AI Unit may equate to processing one document, one process instance, or one recommendation — depending on the use case.

  3. No Need to License for All Process Manager Users

    • You do not need to buy AI units for every Process Manager license.
    • AI Units are independent of the number of users and are tied to the actual usage of AI features.
    • So only users engaging with AI-powered features will consume units.

  4. Estimation and Purchase

    • SAP will assess your use case (e.g., how many processes you want to analyze, frequency, data volume).
    • Based on that, they will suggest an initial bundle (e.g., 10,000 AI Units) with a corresponding price.

  5. Pricing

    • Exact cost per AI Unit is not publicly listed and depends on your region, SAP contract, and bundle size.
    • As a general reference, AI Unit pricing can range from a few cents to several euros per unit, depending on usage tier and scope.

Next Steps

  • Contact your SAP account manager or SAP Signavio customer success partner.
  • Request a usage estimate and pricing model for AI Units based on your intended scenarios (e.g., process mining, variant analysis, benchmarking).
  • Evaluate whether Signavio Process Intelligence or Signavio Process Insights is more aligned with your goals, as both use AI Units differently.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: I can not open Service Binding in Eclipse
entry.content:
Dear Etoril

The error you're encountering:

java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "…bindingTypelnfoSectionPart.getSection()" because "this.bindingTypelnfoSectionPart" is null

is a known issue that occurs in certain versions of Eclipse ADT (ABAP Development Tools) when working with Service Bindings in the SAP BTP ABAP Environment.

Root Cause:

The error typically indicates that the ADT plug-in is either:

  • Outdated and missing support for the specific UI section used in Service Bindings.
  • Corrupted during installation or update.

Recommended Resolution Steps:

1. Update Eclipse ADT Plug-in

Even if you don’t have privileges to update Eclipse, this is the most critical step. You’ll need to:

  • Ask your system administrator to update ADT to the latest version (available via the Eclipse Marketplace or via SAP).
  • Minimum required version: ADT 3.31 or higher is typically recommended for working smoothly with BTP ABAP Environment features.

2. Workaround: Use Eclipse on Another Machine

If updating is not possible on your current environment:

  • Install a fresh version of Eclipse + ADT (latest) on another local machine where you have admin rights.
  • Connect to your BTP ABAP Environment system and re-attempt the Service Binding publish from there.

3. Report to IT Admin

Share the exact error and recommend that they update:

  • Eclipse IDE to a supported version (e.g., Eclipse 2023-12 or newer).
  • ADT tools via https://tools.hana.ondemand.com/latest.

Summary

You cannot fix this error from within Eclipse unless you're allowed to update or reinstall the ADT plug-in. This is a tooling issue, not a backend problem. Updating ADT is required to resolve it.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: For certain materials, orders should only be allowed...
entry.content:
Hello @Mahavir 

Thanks for sharing the requirement—this is something you can handle in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition using in-app extensibility.

  1. Storing the Order Multiple

There isn’t a standard field in the material master for something like “order multiple,” but you can easily create a custom field for this using the Custom Fields and Logic app. You’d add it to the Product (I_Product) business context and make sure it’s available in the sales order process where it’s needed.

  1. Validating During Sales Order Entry

To check the quantity during sales order creation, you can use the BAdI SD_SALES_ITEM_MODIFY. With this, you can write a simple check that compares the entered quantity with the allowed multiple (which you've stored in the material master). If it doesn’t match, the system can pop up a warning message—just as you described.

For example:
If the user enters 13 and the allowed multiple is 5, the system would show a message like:

“Entered quantity is not a multiple of 5.”

This would be a warning, so it won’t block the process but will alert the user.

Optional:

If you want a more flexible setup (e.g., defining multiples by material group or region), you could use Business Rules, but for material-level control, the custom field + BAdI route is cleaner.

How KBAs Apply

  • KBA 2797897 helps ensure your SD_SALES_ITEM_MODIFY implementation won’t fail due to incorrect order-type filtering.
  • KBA 3619567 ensures your new OrderMultiple field is visible and usable in the Create/Change Sales Order apps.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Business Add-Ins

With kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: Re: SAP Build and ABAP Cloud Basic Trial Offering
entry.content:
just for those who needs this info:

I'm on Linux Mint. I was using Eclipse 2024-06 version. But Eclipse couldn't resolve the link which is redirected by SAP Build Lobby. First tried to update ADT but doesnt work! Then update Eclipse to the latest version works!

In my case I couldnt check the box first "Handle Links", because after download Eclipse to Downloads/ folder and install, it will create 2 desktop launcher with ".desktop" extension. One of them use "eclipse+command" (you'll see this in tutorial) feature so you can not check it.

to be able to check "Handle Links" checkbox, on Linux, first you need to remove /.local/share/applications/*eclipse*.desktop (you have 2 desktop launcher, remove one of them) I removed the launcher that includes "Downloads" word in its name.

have a nice life and afterlifeall ABAPers

entry.title: SAP Managed RAP based on Header and Item table
entry.content:
Steps:

1. Create Data Base table for PO Header.

@EndUserText.label : 'PO Header'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zpo_header {

  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  key pono   : ebeln not null;
  pocompany  : bukrs;
  podate     : abap.dats;
  podesc     : abap.char(20);
  postatus   : abap.char(1);

}

2. Create PO Item Database table.

@EndUserText.label : 'PO Item'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zpo_item {

  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  @AbapCatalog.foreignKey.screenCheck : false
  key pono   : ebeln not null
    with foreign key [1..*,1] zpo_header
      where client = zpo_item.client
        and pono = zpo_item.pono;
  key poitem : ebelp not null;
  @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : 'zpo_item.unit'
  quantity   : abap.quan(10,0);
  unit       : abap.unit(3);

}

3. Create Base CDS Header view.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Base view for po'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{ 
serviceQuality: #X, 
sizeCategory: #S, 
dataClass: #MIXED 
} 

define root view entity z_po_header_base as select from zpo_header
{
    key pono as Pono,
    pocompany as Pocompany,
    podate as Podate,
    podesc as Podesc,
    postatus as Postatus
    
}

4. Create Base CDS item view.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Base view for po item'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity z_po_item_base as select from zpo_item

{
    key pono as Pono,
    key poitem as Poitem,
    @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : 'Unit'
    quantity as Quantity,
    unit as Unit
}

5. Create Composite Header CDS view.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Composit view for po'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define root  view entity z_po_header_composit as select from z_po_header_base
 composition [1..*] of z_po_item_composit as _item
   
{
    key Pono,
    Pocompany,
    Podate,
    Podesc,
    Postatus,
    _item
}

6. Create Composite Item CDS view.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Composit view for po item'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity z_po_item_composit as select from z_po_item_base
association to parent z_po_header_composit as _header
    on $projection.Pono = _header.Pono
{
    key Pono,
    key Poitem,
     @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : 'Unit'
   Quantity,
    Unit,
    _header 
}

 

7. Create Consumption CDS Header view.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Consumption view for po'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define root view entity z_po_header_consumption  
provider contract transactional_query
as projection on z_po_header_composit
{
    key Pono,
    Pocompany,
    Podate,
    Podesc,
    Postatus,
    /* Associations */
    _item : redirected to composition child z_po_item_consumption 
}

8. Create Consumption Item CDS view.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Consumption view for po item'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity z_po_item_consumption 
as projection on z_po_item_composit

{
    key Pono,
    key Poitem,
 
   @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : 'Unit'
    Quantity,
    Unit,
    /* Associations */
    _header : redirected to parent z_po_header_consumption
}

9. Create Metadata Extension for Header.

@Metadata.layer: #CUSTOMER 
annotate entity z_po_header_consumption
    with 
{
    @ui.facet: [{ 
purpose: #STANDARD, 
position: 10, 
label: 'Header', 
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE 
}, 
{ 
purpose: #STANDARD, 
position: 20, 
label: 'Item', 
type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE, 
targetElement: '_item' 
}] 
@UI.selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'PO No' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 10, label: 'Po NO' }] 
Pono; 
@UI.selectionField: [{ position: 20 }]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Company' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Company' }] 
Pocompany; 
@UI.selectionField: [{ position: 30 }]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'PO Date' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'PO Date' }] 
Podate; 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 40, label: 'Desc' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Desc' }] 
Podesc; 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 50, label: 'Status' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 50, label: 'Status' }] 
Postatus; 
    
}

10.  Create Metadata extension for Item .

@Metadata.layer: #CUSTOMER
annotate entity z_po_item_consumption
    with 
{
    @ui.facet: [{ 
purpose: #STANDARD, 
position: 10, 
label: 'Item', 
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE 
}] 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Po no' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 10, label: 'Po no' }] 
Pono; 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Item' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Item' }] 
Poitem; 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Quantity' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'Quantity' }] 
Quantity; 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position:40, label: 'Unit' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Unit' }] 
Unit; 
    
}

11. Create Behavior Definition of Composite view.

managed implementation in class zbp_po_header_composit unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for z_po_header_composit //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zpo_header
lock master
authorization master ( instance )
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  association _item { create; }
}

define behavior for z_po_item_composit //alias <alias_name>
persistent table zpo_item
lock dependent by _header
authorization dependent by _header
//etag master <field_name>
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) Pono;
  association _header;
}

12. Create Projection Behavior Definition.

projection;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for z_po_header_consumption //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;

  use association _item { create; }
}

define behavior for z_po_item_consumption //alias <alias_name>
{
  use update;
  use delete;

  use association _header;
}

13. Create Service Definition.

@EndUserText.label: 'Service Definition fo PO Header'
define service Z_SV_PO_HEADER {
  expose z_po_header_consumption;
  expose z_po_item_consumption;
}

14. Create Service Binding.

 

15. Now Application can be run from Preview. Header and Item can be created .

16. Records created in Header and Item table.

entry.title: Hello Miss. Doris, group reporting preparation ledger config does it have to be : SAP standard confi
entry.content: Hello Miss. entry.title: 🌟Unlock Insights Instantly: How to Configure KPI Cards in SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
Keeping track of performance metrics shouldn't require navigating across multiple apps or digging into reports. With SAP’s KPI Cards on the Home Page, administrators can now offer end-users a personalized dashboard that pulls real-time data from various applications—right at their fingertips.

In this blog, we’ll walk through how you, as an administrator, can create and configure KPI cards to help your users stay focused on what truly matters.

🎯Why KPI Cards?

KPI (Key Performance Indicator) Cards give users a quick snapshot of essential metrics like sales Quotes  and opportunities. These cards are highly configurable and serve as visual indicators to drive data-informed decisions.

🔧Prerequisite: Enable the KPI Cards Feature

Before you start, make sure KPI Cards are enabled in your system.

📌How to enable:
Raise a case under the component CEC-CRM-PM-PF with the subject:"Enable Creation of KPI Cards" 

🧭 Step-by-Step: Create Your KPI Cards

Once the feature is enabled, here’s how to set up KPI cards on your SAP Home Page:

  1. 🏠Enter Adaptation Mode

    • Navigate to your Home Page.
    • Open the User Menu and select Start Adaptation.
    • An orange bar will appear at the top, indicating that you're now in adaptation mode.

  2. 🧩 Access the Card Editor

    • Hover over the border of the My Cards section.
    • Click the pencil icon (Edit) to open the Create Cards pop-up.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  • ➕Select a New KPI Card – Click on the “+” (Create Card) button to start creating a new KPI card.
  • 🧭 Access Tools and Applications – Once selected, the "Tools and Applications" section will appear.
  • 📂Choose Application – From the list, select the application that contains the data source for your KPI.

 

  • 📊View Card Types – You will now see options for Standard Cards and KPI Cards.
  • ✅Select KPI Cards – From these, choose “KPI Cards” to start configuring a performance metric card.

 

 

  • ➕Add Available KPI Cards – You’ll now see available options like Leads and Quotes under the KPI Cards section.
  • 🖱️ Click the “+” Button – Select the cards you want to add by clicking the “+” icon next to each, then click Apply to confirm.

 

 

  • ✏️Customize the KPI Card – Hover over the newly added card and click the pencil icon to edit.
  • ⚙️Edit KPI Card Settings – In the Edit KPI Card dialog:
  • 🔽Use the Saved Filters dropdown to apply a predefined filter.
  • 📊Select the relevant metric from the KPI Fields dropdown.
  • 📈Choose your preferred Graph Type (e.g., bar, line, donut, etc.).
  • 💾Save Configuration – Click Apply to save your customized KPI card setup.

  • 🚪Exit Adaptation Mode – Once all configurations are complete, click End Session to exit adaptation mode.
  • 🏠View Your Dashboard – Your personalized KPI card is now live and visible on your SAP Home Page for real-time insights.

🧩 Use Cases

  • 👨‍💼Sales Managers – Easily track pipeline performance and conversion metrics in real-time.
  • 🛠️ Support Leads – Monitor open ticket volumes, resolution times, and service efficiency at a glance.
  • 📊Executives – Instantly view top-level KPIs like revenue, growth, or customer satisfaction on the homepage.

✅Final Thoughts

With just a few clicks, you can transform the SAP Home Page into a personalized dashboard that surfaces critical metrics for your team or organization. Whether you're focused on operational performance, customer service, or sales growth, KPI Cards offer a clean, visual, and customizable way to stay informed.

entry.title: Re: Error "Money Overflow; cannot display all digits"
entry.content:
Hi

The error "Money Overflow; cannot display all digits" in SAP Business One usually points to a numeric overflow issue. It indicates that a field value has exceeded the allowed number of digits that can be stored or displayed, typically due to extremely high values.

In your case, since it occurs only in one specific bin location, the likely root cause is:

The quantity or monetary value of the stock in that bin has reached a number that exceeds the system's capacity to display or calculate (for example, too many decimal places or an extremely large total value due to cumulative transactions or incorrect entries).

Steps to resolve:

  1. Identify the bin location ID that triggers the error.

  2. Use SQL queries in the database to inspect the OIBQ and OBIN tables for that bin location. Look at the OnHandQty or AvgPrice fields and check if they contain abnormal values.

  3. Run the Inventory Audit Report or Inventory Valuation Report filtered by bin and item to identify the problematic entry.

  4. Check if there's a negative or excessively high average price or quantity.

  5. If data corruption is confirmed, work with your SAP partner or support to correct the data directly in the database or through SAP-provided tools/scripts.

Until corrected, avoid inventory operations with that bin location. If needed urgently, you can temporarily block that bin from transactions using bin restrictions.

Best regards

PO price is changed after MIGO, and during MIRO, the difference due to PO price change triggers the posting to EIN/EKG, and system fills the Profitability segments PSG number to these two EIN/EKG lines, then the error message KI280 pops up when we attempts to post.

entry.title: Re: OBYC-EIN EKG G/L accounts shows message KI280 during...
entry.content:
Hi

The error KI280 in MIRO is caused because the system tries to post to a G/L account (EIN/EKG) with a profitability segment (PSG) in a scenario where the account is not defined as a cost element. In S/4HANA with Margin Analysis activated, whenever a profitability segment is involved in the accounting line, the system expects the G/L account to be a cost element, especially for postings with PSG attributes.

In your case, during MIGO everything works fine because no profitability segment is triggered at that point. However, during MIRO, due to the price change after goods receipt, the system determines that the difference should be posted to the EIN/EKG account and at the same time it fills a PSG number because of the sales order linkage and Margin Analysis.

Since the EIN/EKG G/L accounts are not created as cost elements in transaction KA01 or via app "Manage Cost Elements", and because PSG is filled, the system throws error KI280 indicating that it cannot post to this account with a PSG.

To resolve this:

  1. Either convert the affected G/L accounts used for EIN and EKG into cost elements (typically of category 1 - Primary cost element), which allows PSG postings.
  2. Or avoid the profitability segment assignment for these lines, which might require adjusting account determination or the configuration of the relevant posting logic in the system to bypass PSG derivation for these accounts.

Option 1 is recommended if PSG-based analysis is expected and you want to allow the system to post price differences with profitability segments. Ensure the account is not restricted from PSG in its field status or derivation rules.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: SAP Commerce Cloud, Composable Storefront Support wit...
entry.content:
Dear Adam

YES. SAP Commerce Cloud Composable Storefront will maintain compatibility with the August 2025 Core Commerce release for the same 12-month period and not revert to the typical six-month release cycle in this cas.

SAP has extended the August 2025 release of SAP Commerce Cloud Core Commerce to 12 months of mainstream maintenance, instead of the usual six-month update cycle.

SAP Composable Storefront, formerly known as Spartacus, has been following an aligned versioning and support strategy with Core Commerce since early 2024. This alignment includes synchronizing release timing, compatibility, and support duration. Composable Storefront receives monthly updates for features and bug fixes, and a yearly Angular framework upgrade typically takes place in February. In addition, bugfix-only periods are observed during December and January.

Because the August 2025 Core Commerce release benefits from a 12-month support window, the Composable Storefront release aligned to it will also maintain compatibility for the full 12-month period. This ensures customers can rely on long-term compatibility and coordinated maintenance across both the Core Commerce backend and the Composable frontend.

Therefore, the answer is yes — SAP Commerce Cloud Composable Storefront will maintain compatibility with the August 2025 Core Commerce release for the same 12-month period and not revert to the typical six-month release cycle in this cas

  • Best regards

entry.title: Re: SAP Build and ABAP Cloud Basic Trial Offering
entry.content:
It seems that you have misunderstood something.

Once you have changed the code in the behavior definition such

managed with additional save implementation in class ZBP_R_AC000000U## unique;

You will be able to add the implementation of an empty saver class using Ctrl+1.

The empty saver class will look as follows, and if you look closely at the code snippets for the implementation of the method save_modified(), you won't see any usage of "failed". 

Kind regards,

Andre

 

CLASS lsc_zr_ac000000u## DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver.

  PROTECTED SECTION.

    METHODS save_modified REDEFINITION.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lsc_zr_ac000000u## IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD save_modified.
 
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

entry.title: Actual Activity Rate Calculation without using Material Ledger Actual Costing (33Q)
entry.content:
Question:
In a "Make to stock" production accounting scenario, where the Scope Item 33Q(Actual Costing) is not activated, how do we calculate the "Actual Activity rates" and post "Activity Price variances" on Price Difference Account?

(SAP S4HANA Public Cloud)

Detail:

If actual Costing (33Q) is NOT activated, and the scenario is as follows:

  1. It’s a Make to Stock Scenario  where the Finished Good and the components (RM and SFG) are all valuated using the Price Control "S".
  • When the goods are issued to the Production Order, the posting is done based on the Standard Price of the Materials. To be precise: "Actual Material Quantity * Standard Price".
  • When the Activity Confirmation happens on the Production Order, the posting is done based on the valuation variant define in the Production Order type. In our case it is the Planned Period Rates for Activities. Thus, "Actual Activity Quantity * Planned Activity Rate".

 

As we are not running Actual Costing (CKMLCP equivalent) because 33Q is out of scope, I completely understand that the material price variances due to the RM/SFG material price differences are not going to be settled on to the Finished good, as we do not have the Actual Costing activated. Thus, material price variances will remain posted on the price difference account (PRD) for the Raw/SFG Materials.

 

But I would like to understand, how the Activity Price variance (due to the difference between Planned & Actual Activity Rates) are going to be calculated, and captured on the price difference accounts?

entry.title: Re: Commit work in exit Workflow

entry.title: Re: Quarterly Reporting in S4/HANA Group Reporting Public...
entry.content:
Dear Geeta

Have you tried this?

 

  1. Use of FS Items and Group Reporting Data Collection: Ensure that all financial data is correctly posted and consolidated using ACDOCU.

  2. Reporting Tool: Use SAP Group Reporting Data Collection (GRDC) or SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) as a front-end for reporting, depending on your landscape. Both tools support parameter prompts and dynamic filtering.

  3. Time Selection with Variable Prompts:

    • In SAC, create input prompts or variables for Period, Quarter, and Year. You can implement a logic with restricted measures or calculated measures that adapt based on the selection (e.g. if QTD is selected, sum values from the beginning of the quarter to the current month).
    • In Group Reporting Fiori apps, like "Group Data Analysis", you can create a variant or custom report where period values are dynamically filtered based on the prompt selection using filters and parameters (FISCVAR, FISCYEAR, PERIOD).

  4. Restricted Key Figures: Define restricted key figures for Monthly, QTD, and YTD in the report. You can create:

    • One for Monthly = selected period only
    • One for QTD = sum of periods 1 to selected period within the quarter
    • One for YTD = sum from period 1 to selected period in the year

  5. Custom Logic (if needed): If more complex logic is required, develop a custom CDS View with logic for QTD, using CASE statements and PERIOD filters to compute cumulative figures depending on the quarter.

  6. Optional: If using BW/4HANA or embedded analytics, time-dependent hierarchies and custom structures can be used to provide a similar time navigation experience.

Best regards

entry.title: Debugging a Python SAP BTP Service Locally in VSCode
entry.content:
There is a lot of information available about creating and debugging Node.js services in SAP BTP. However, in some cases, we might need to use Python instead — for example, for tasks involving AI or data processing.

In this article, I’ll show you how to debug a Python-based SAP BTP service using VS Code locally.

Prerequisites

Create an SAP BTP Trial Account. Please follow this tutorial for detailed information. 

Add HANA to your SAP BTP trial account if you haven’t already.

To do this, refer to the section ‘Create an SAP HANA Cloud Instance’ of this tutorial.

Note: One of the steps in the tutorial above requires the Space ID.
If you're not sure where to find it, navigate to your space in the SAP BTP Cockpit and copy the Space ID from the URL.

Install Visual Studio Code (VS Code). You can download it from this page.

Install the SAP Cloud Foundry CLI. Refer to this guide on installing the cf CLI for detailed instructions.

Install the Python interpreter. Version 3.13 or higher is recommended.

Install the necessary extensions for VS Code:

  • Python extension by Microsoft
  • Python debugger extension by Microsoft

Note: When installing the first extension (Python), the second one (Python Debugger) may be installed automatically. If not, you can install it manually.

Steps to create a service

Our service will not include authorization for the sake of simplicity, but it will use an SAP HANA service to demonstrate how to call a BTP service from the local environment. We will use the initial setup from this tutorial.

In the following sections, I will replicate some of the steps from that tutorial in this blog post, with a few minor adjustments.

  • In your local file system, create a new folder –  for example, python-debug.
  • In Visual Studio Code, open the python-debug folder.
  • Create a file named manifest.yml with the following content:
---
applications:
  - name: python-debug
    random-route: true
    path: ./
    memory: 128M
    buildpacks:
      - python_buildpack
    command: python server.py
    services:
      - python-debug-hana
​

IMPORTANT: Make sure you don’t already have another application named python-debug in your space. If you do, use a different name and update the entire tutorial accordingly.

  • Specify the Python runtime version your application will use. To do this, create a file named runtime.txt with the following content:
python-3.13.x​
  • This application will be a web server utilizing the Flask web framework. To specify Flask as a dependency, create a file named requirements.txt with the following content:
Flask==2.3.*
cfenv==0.5.3
hdbcli==2.17.*
  • Create a file named server.py with the following application logic:
import os
from flask import Flask
from cfenv import AppEnv
from hdbcli import dbapi

app = Flask(__name__)
env = AppEnv()

hana_service = 'hana'
hana = env.get_service(label=hana_service)

port = int(os.environ.get('PORT', 3000))
@app.route('/')
def hello():
    if hana is None:
        return "Can't connect to HANA service '{}' – check service name?".format(hana_service)
    else:
        conn = dbapi.connect(address=hana.credentials['host'],
                             port=int(hana.credentials['port']),
                             user=hana.credentials['user'],
                             password=hana.credentials['password'],
                             encrypt='true',
                             sslTrustStore=hana.credentials['certificate'])

        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute("select CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP from DUMMY")
        ro = cursor.fetchone()
        cursor.close()
        conn.close()

        return "Current time is: " + str(ro["CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP"])

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=port)
​

This is a simple server that will return the result of a basic HANA SQL query when requested (in our case, the current UTC time).

  • Log in to Cloud Foundry using the following command in the command line:
cf login​

When prompted, enter your API endpoint. You can find it on the Account Overview page in your SAP BTP account.

Also, enter the registration email and password for your SAP BTP trial account.

  • Create a HANA service using the following command:
cf create-service hana hdi-shared python-debug-hana​
  • Deploy the application on Cloud Foundry. To do that, run the following command inside the python-debug directory:
cf push​

Note: If you make any changes to the service file and want to deploy again, simply run the ‘cf push’ command again.

  • After successful deployment, the python-debug application should start automatically, and its details will be displayed in the command console.
  • Open a browser and enter the application’s generated URL (you can find it under “routes”)
    For example: python-debug-zany-buffalo-zz.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com

Steps to debug the service locally

Local debugging of applications running on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) is not only possible, but also practical in many cases. Here’s why:

  • Globally Accessible Services:
    In SAP BTP, all service endpoints use globally routable addresses. This means that your application can access these services from any location, not just from within the SAP BTP environment. As a result, you can run and test your application locally while still interacting with cloud services such as databases, messaging queues, or authentication providers.
  • Minimal Environmental Differences:
    SAP BTP does not provide a highly customized runtime environment. Apart from environment variables—which you can replicate locally—there are no special runtime conditions. This allows developers to simulate the production environment with high fidelity on their local machines by simply setting the appropriate environment variables.

Thanks to these two factors, local development and debugging in SAP BTP is straightforward. It enables faster development cycles and easier troubleshooting without the need to redeploy for every change.

To enable local debugging of a Python-based service, follow these steps:

  • Since the SAP BTP environment provides necessary configuration through environment variables, you need to replicate them locally. In order to load local environment data, insert the following changed code to your Python server script:
import os
from flask import Flask
from cfenv import AppEnv
from hdbcli import dbapi
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()

app = Flask(__name__)
env = AppEnv()

hana_service = 'hana'
hana = env.get_service(label=hana_service)

port = int(os.environ.get('PORT', 3000))
@app.route('/')
def hello():
    if hana is None:
        return "Can't connect to HANA service '{}' – check service name?".format(hana_service)
    else:
        conn = dbapi.connect(address=hana.credentials['host'],
                             port=int(hana.credentials['port']),
                             user=hana.credentials['user'],
                             password=hana.credentials['password'],
                             encrypt='true',
                             sslTrustStore=hana.credentials['certificate'])

        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute("select CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP from DUMMY")
        ro = cursor.fetchone()
        cursor.close()
        conn.close()

        return "Current time is: " + str(ro["CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP"])

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=port)
​

This new logic will let you automatically read the key-value pairs from your .env file and make them available via os.environ, just like in the cloud environment.

  • Update the requirements.txt to include the necessary libraries
Flask==2.3.*
cfenv==0.5.3
hdbcli==2.17.*
python-dotenv​
  • Create a Python Virtual Environment

    Every Python project should run in its own isolated environment to avoid dependency conflicts.

    In VS Code, open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P), and choose ‘Python: Create Environment…’
  • Then select the option ‘Venv Creates a ‘.venv’ virtual environment in the current workspace’:
  • Then select a Python Version (Python 3.13.x or higher is recommended):
  • On the next screen, confirm the option to install the dependencies:

Note: You can also do this later using the command pip install -r requirements.txt.

  • A new virtual environment will be created in a .venv folder in your workspace:
  • Set the VCAP_SERVICES environment variable
    Before running the service locally, we need to set the VCAP_SERVICES environment variable, which provides all the necessary information about SAP BTP services.
    To retrieve its contents, use the following command:
cf env python-debug​

Now, copy the contents of the VCAP_SERVICES JSON block to your clipboard.

  • Before we can use the contents of the environment variable, we need to minimize the JSON into a single line. You can use any online JSON minifier tool or the JSTool extension in Notepad++ to do this.
  • Now, create a .env file in your service folder (the same folder where the manifest.yml file is located), and add the environment variable contents to it in the following format:
VCAP_SERVICES=<minimized_json>​

  • Create a configuration in VSCode. Before starting, open the server.py file in the editor.
  • Open the ‘Run and Debug’ section in VSCode and select ‘Create a launch.json file’:

    Note: If you have already created the configuration file, simply open it for edit.

  • From the drop-down list, select ‘Python debugger’
  • Select ‘Python File: Debug the currently active Python file’:
  • In the created file, delete the commented-out lines starting with // to avoid syntax errors
  • Return to the server.py file, and start the debugging session
  • Start the debug session
  • The debugging session is now running:
  • Open the following address in a browser
http://127.0.0.1/​

The current date and time from the HANA server will be displayed

  • Then, return to VSCode and set a break-point
  • Refresh the browser again, and the execution will pause at the breakpoint:

Now, you can use all the Python debugger’s features to debug your program.

entry.title: Re: Actual Activity Rate Calculation without using Materi...
entry.content:
Hello @KaranChauhan 

Thank you for your detailed question regarding the calculation of Actual Activity Rates and treatment of Activity Price Variances in your Make-to-Stock production scenario, specifically where Scope Item 33Q (Actual Costing) is not activated.

I’d like to confirm the following, based on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition standard functionality

System Behaviour without 33Q (Actual Costing)

  • Your materials, including Finished Goods and Components (RM/SFG), are valued with Price Control’s (Standard Price).
  • During production:
    • Goods Issue is posted as Actual Quantity × Standard Price.
    • Activity Confirmation posts as Actual Activity Quantity × Planned Activity Rate.

As you rightly mentioned, without 33Q, there is no Material Ledger Actual Costing (CKMLCP equivalent), and inventory remains valued at standard price throughout.

Treatment of Activity Price Variances

  • You continue to plan Activity Rates via your Cost Center/Activity Type Planning.
  • At period-end, SAP compares actual costs incurred on cost centers against the actual activity quantities confirmed during production.
  • The system calculates an actual activity rate based on this comparison.

The difference between Planned vs. Actual Activity Rates results in an Activity Price Variance, which Is posted to the Price Difference Account (PRD)

- Does not adjust inventory valuation, as Actual Costing (33Q) is not in scope
- Remains visible within financial reporting as a variance impact on P&L

Note

  • Without 33Q
    • Both Material Price Variances and Activity Price Variances are posted to variance (PRD) accounts.
    • There is no inventory revaluation based on actual costs.
    • This approach follows SAP's standard for Public Cloud when Actual Costing is excluded from the project scope.

SAP Help Portal – Product Costing and Overhead Management

SAP KBA 3086829 – Actual Activity Rate Behaviour in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

KBA 3086829 – Actual Activity Rate & Variances in Public Cloud

Key Takeaways

 In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, if Actual Costing (33Q) is not activated:

  • Materials remain valued at Standard Price (S).
  • Activity confirmations use the Planned Activity Rate set in Cost Center/Activity Type Planning.

 At Period-End, the system

  • Compares Planned Activity Rates used during production with actual costs incurred on Cost Centers.
  • Calculates an Actual Activity Rate based on real costs and confirmed activity quantities.

 Variance Handling

  • Differences between Planned and Actual Activity Rates result in Activity Price Variances.
  • These variances are posted to:
    • The Price Difference Account (PRD) for materials.
    • Or remain within the Cost Center/Profitability structure for non-material linked activities.

Without 33Q

  • There is no inventory revaluation, regardless of variances.
  • All variances stay in P&L accounts.

Practical Example

  • You plan Activity Rate = €70/hour
  • Actual costs on Cost Center result in Actual Activity Rate = €75/hour
  • Variance (€5/hour) posted to PRD at period-end — inventory stays valued at standard price

Conclusion

  • This is standard Public Cloud behavior, designed for scenarios where full Material Ledger Actual Costing is not in scope.
  • Variances are visible in financial reports but do not impact stock valuation.

Let me know if you need further support

entry.title: Re: How to get material number from KSSK table
entry.content:
Tried this using SQVI and worked . The missing table for me was INOB .This is what I did : 

Step 1: Get CLINT from KLAH

  • Go to table KLAH
  • Enter your batch class name in CLASS
  • Ensure KLART = 023 (023 = Batch class)
  • Note down the CLINT value

 

Step 2: Go to table KSSK

  • Enter:
    • CLINT = value from Step 1
    • KLART = 023
  • This gives you a list of internal object numbers (KSSK-OBJEK) linked to that class

Step 3: Go to table INOB

  • Enter CUOBJ = KSSK-OBJEK
  • Filter:
    • KLART = 023
    • OBTAB = MARA
  • INOB-OBJEK will give you the material numbers

entry.title: Re: HTTP Content Server all communication via SAP server?
entry.content:
Hello, 

   If you want all the requests to the content server to go via SAP Application Server,  You make sure that RFC destination 'SAPHTTP' is not accessible. You can also try user parameter 'SAPHTTP_RFC_CHECK_TO' VALUE 'FAILED' This will make sure that SAPHTTP destination will not be called for a that user.

Regards

SSB

entry.title: Re: Custom MDF Analysis Dimension problem - Neuroinclusion
entry.content:
Hi @DariusSoreata - Lookups tab is provided in you need to map table without User ID or Person ID available to other table that has User ID/Person ID available.  There are examples already in customer configuration that you can check.  If External Code is not User ID or Person ID, do not just setup this field as Primary Person or Secondary Person.   Primary Person or Secondary Person special fields need to be consistent, i.e. if User ID is setup as Primary Person then other Primary Person should only contain User ID value, otherwise it will cause issue during processing.

You need to understand the MDF tables and how they link to user related records in Job Info.  This I cannot help as you need to find out yourself.  Then use Lookups tab to link these tables together.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Not able to delete or remove an Exclusion Rules from...
entry.content:
Dear Requester,

Once the rule is activated into Active status, it is not possible to delete or remove it. The button of Remove is grayed out.

Removal of a rule is possible when the rule is In Preparation status.

If the exclusion rule is activated and then not needed, set it to Blocked status by clicking Action button then choose Block Rule option.

Note, once the rule is Blocked status, no more action could be done to it, which means the rule can not be reverted back to Active status.

 

Thanks,

Adie

entry.title: Re: Time confirmation with two assign 2 activity types to...
entry.content: Hi Ndubuisi_PALMER, Thank you for your response. We share the same perspective, and it has been confirmed. However, it seems that SAP did not fully consider scenarios where unplanned activity types or work centers might need to confirm the same order operation. In IW41, you can post confirmations for a specific activity type as long as the associated work center is entered. For example, activity types A and B can each have their own work centers, and the system allows this. However, in the FIORI app Find Maintenance Order and Operations with the mass upload time confirmation feature, the upload template only includes the activity type column—there is no field for the work center. This limitation prevents us from performing the type of confirmations that are possible in the SAP GUI.

entry.title: Limit Item not show time period in PO
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support,

I am currently working with limit items in S/4HANA Public Cloud, which have replaced framework orders from ECC 6.0. In ECC, we were able to maintain a validity period (start and end dates) at the PO header level when converting a framework-type purchase requisition to a PO. This functionality allowed us to monitor timelines and extract the validity period into the PO output form.

Howevr, in the Public Cloud version, while the limit amount is shown at the item level, the validity dates are no longer displayed at the header level.

Since I need to define a timeline to monitor framework-type POs and also reflect this information in the PO form output, could you please help verify where this data can be maintained or if there is an alternative approach in the Public Cloud?

Thank you for your support.

 

Best regards,
Keratiya D.
